data.title: India Gold Market Update: Rally And Demand Realignment
data.description:
Looking ahead

  • Seasonal and wedding related purchases may support gold demand this month and next. Investor interest is likely to strengthen as gold’s appeal as a safe-haven asset and portfolio diversifier heightens amid global economic uncertainties and financial market volatility.

Gold extends rally and breaks new thresholds

Gold has continued its impressive rally, repeatedly setting new record highs across major currencies and recently surpassing the threshold of US$3,200/oz.1 A weaker USD and heightened geopolitical risk – including tariff-led fear and uncertainty – have been key drivers of gold’s performance. Alongside this, strong gold ETF buying across most regions has been propelling gold higher.

Chart 1: Golden surge continues

Monthly LBMA Price AM and domestic spot price changes and movement*

So far in 20252 the LBMA gold price PM in USD has climbed by US$621/oz or 24%, to US$3,230/oz, with over 14% of this increase occurring since March. The Indian domestic spot gold prices3 have mirrored this trend, rising 23% y-t-d to INR93,217/10g. For much of the past four months domestic prices have traded at a discount to international benchmarks (after adjusting for exchange rate and taxes),4 primarily due to subdued jewellery demand amid elevated prices. While domestic prices briefly moved into a slight premium in early April, the average discount has widened significantly – from US$12/oz in mid-March to over US$30/oz on 11 April.5

Gold buyers turn selective

Gold's steep climb and ongoing volatility are keeping many consumers on the sidelines, with demand for jewellery continuing to be limited to need based purchases, particularly for weddings. There has been a noticeable shift in consumer behaviour in response to soaring prices, with more buyers opting to trade in old jewellery for new: anecdotal reports suggest that 40–45% of purchases now involve some form of exchange.

While festive buying has continued, it remains modest and localised, often tied to specific regions and communities. At the same time, the investment appeal of gold is gaining prominence. Anecdotal reports suggest that demand for bars and coins has been resilient, even at high price levels.

The trend of festival and wedding related purchases is likely to persist, supported by the safe-haven appeal of gold. But this may not offset the drop in discretionary purchases. Amid broader financial market turmoil and uncertainty, gold's role as a store of value is becoming more pronounced, reflecting a shift in consumer behaviour from consumption-driven purchases to wealth preservation.

Stronger quarter for corporate retail jewellers

Earnings reports from leading jewellery retailers6 for January-March quarter point to a strong performance, with average revenue growth up 25–35% y/y. This was largely fuelled by wedding related demand, festive buying, and a noticeable consumer shift toward gold – both as an adornment and a store of value.

While high gold prices tempered demand at the lower price points, the premium segments remained fairly resilient. Average transaction values reportedly rose by 15% to 20% y/y. Also, there was an uptick in old gold exchanges, which contributed to overall sales volumes. Corporate retailers7 continued their aggressive store expansion strategy, adding 10 to 30 new outlets during the quarter and reinforcing the growing presence of organised players in the jewellery sector.

These large retailers remain upbeat about the outlook for the April-June quarter. Early indicators of advance bookings for regional festivals such as Akshaya Tritiya – a key period for gold purchases – point to strong consumer sentiment. Further underlining this bullish outlook, leading retailers plan to open between 150 and 200 new showrooms over the next 12 months, setting the stage for continued gains in market share and deeper penetration across regions.

Streak snaps: gold ETFs see outflows

In a divergence from the global trend, Indian gold ETFs recorded modest net outflows in March, following ten consecutive months of sustained, strong inflows. Anecdotal evidence suggests that the pullback likely reflects profit-taking amid a record rally in gold prices, alongside portfolio rebalancing. Despite the outflows, assets under management (AUM) climbed to a record high, driven by the surge in gold prices, while investor participation continued to grow as new accounts were added.

According to the Association of Mutual Funds in India (AMFI), gold ETFs recorded net outflows of INR0.8bn (~US$8.9mn) in March, the first monthly outflow since April 2024. This is lower than our initial estimates, which were based on preliminary data.8 Despite these outflows, cumulative assets under management (AUM) of gold ETFs rose to INR589bn (~US$6.8bn), up 6% m/m and 89% y/y thanks to the gold price increase. Gold ETFs now account for 0.9% of total mutual fund AUM, up from 0.6% a year ago. The collective gold holdings of the 20 gold ETFs stood at 64.5t, down 0.1t from a month ago. However, strong inflows during the preceding two months added 6.7t to the collective holding in Q1 2025, the highest quarterly addition on record.

Investor interest in this asset class continues to grow with 0.13mn new investor accounts (or folios) added during the month, bringing the total number of gold ETF investor accounts to a record 7mn. In the last twelve months 1.9mn folios were added to gold ETFs.

Chart 2: Gold ETFs hit pause on inflows

Monthly gold ETF fund flows in INRbn, and total holdings in tonnes*

RBI moderates gold buying but gold’s role in reserves strengthens

The RBI added a modest 0.6t of gold to its reserves in March, resuming purchases after a pause in February, according to our estimates based on the central bank’s weekly forex reserve data. This brings the RBI’s total gold holdings to 879.6t, or 11.7% of its total foreign exchange reserves – the highest level both in quantum and share.

Over the past year, gold’s share in the RBI’s forex reserves has risen nearly 4%, reflecting a net addition of 57.5t to its holdings. Yet recent trends suggest a moderation in the central bank’s gold buying. After consistently purchasing an average of 6.6t per month from January to November 2024, the RBI took a breather in December and February. And in January and March its purchases were notably below the previous monthly average.

This recent pattern in gold purchases may suggest a more measured approach from the RBI, although it underscores gold's rising strategic importance in India’s reserves management.

Chart 3: RBI’s gold accumulation slows

RBI’s monthly net purchase and reserves, tonnes *

Turnaround in imports

Despite record high prices, gold imports rebounded sharply in March after two consecutive months of decline. According to data from the Ministry of Commerce, imports climbed to US$4.4bn – nearly double the previous month's figure and significantly higher than the US$1.53bn recorded a year earlier. While still below the average monthly imports of US$7.3bn set between August and December 2024, the sharp uptick suggests a resurgence in demand and underscores a continued interest in gold, even at elevated prices. Based on our estimates, import volumes for the month were in the range of 47t to 52t.

The latest data from the Ministry of Commerce has revised gold imports for 2024 – from 724t to 812t – mainly due to revisions in import figures for the period from July to October.

Chart 4: Gold imports see strongest uptick in three months

Monthly gold imports in tonnes and US$bn*

Footnotes

1On 11 April,2025.

2As of 11 April 2025.

3MCX spot gold price.

4The premium/discount of local gold prices is based on the LBMA Gold Price AM adjusted for import taxes and exchange rate, which is also referred to as the ‘landed price’.

5Based on NCDEX data as 11 April 2025.

6Kalyan Jewellers India Pvt Ltd,, Titian Company Ltd, and Senco Gold Ltd.

7Comprises Kalyan Jewellers India Pvt Ltd,, Titian Company Ltd, and Senco Gold Ltd.

8Our initial estimate based on partial information available at the time was outflows of US$51mn.

data.title: Nextracker: An Opportunity That Shines In The Sun
data.description:
Nextracker Inc (NASDAQ:NXT) is a company dedicated to providing software solutions and solar trackers for solar-powered electric generation and distribution projects. The company operates primarily in the United States, but also has international presence, primarily in Asia and Europe. The company was founded in 2013, so we can say it's still experiencing rapid growth and business model expansion. Even so, the company has shown sustained organic revenue growth.

On the other hand, it is important to contextualize the potential importance of solar energy among alternative energy sources driving future changes in the global energy mix. According to the IEA, the importance of solar energy is expected to increase in the coming years as technology evolves, the number of watts generated becomes more efficient, and costs decrease. In this scenario, Nextracker undoubtedly has a relevant role to play.

Thesis

As an introduction, I'd like to begin by analyzing the solar energy and renewable energy industry in general. In that sense, I'd like to contextualize how it could evolve from the current context and in the future to understand what we can expect from Nextracker in the coming years. I find it important to be able to contextualize where the industry is at, what we can expect in the future, and, once that's done, how Nextracker stands in this context.

I'd like to start with a look at the developments in the energy and solar panel industry in general. Once this is in context, I'll try to outline the future expectations for its development.

Solar Energy Industry Overview

I want to begin by outlining the main characteristics of the current state of the renewable energy industry, and within it the solar energy industry in particular. As can be predicted, the world is expected to converge on a shift in the production matrix to achieve a transition from a predominance of fossil fuels to green energy.

There is a widespread market consensus that the energy transition will be rapid and that humanity will abandon fossil fuels in the short term. This is a view I don't fully agree with, and I believe we will still have to live with oil and its derivatives for a few more decades. Still, it's undeniable that renewable energy production methods and the search for their efficiency are growing. Currently, the main disadvantages are related to two key aspects. First, the efficiency and consistency of these energies.

Unlike oil, in terms of energy and economic efficiency, some of the main renewable energies are often unmatched. On the one hand, because they are not stable in terms of energy production, and on the other, because they are in stages of development and research that still need to be explored before they can fully exploit their potential. Something in which human ingenuity clearly plays a fundamental role, and which we will be able to see with the passage of time and at an accelerated pace.

The other is transportation. Transporting crude oil, which can then be used to generate energy, is relatively easy and cheap. Alternative forms of energy such as wind or solar power don't offer this advantage with current technology. It's not surprising, therefore, that efforts are being made to perfect the technique and efficiency of battery construction to make transportation cost-effective and easy.

That said, and without prejudice to the foregoing, it is clear that humanity will sooner or later abandon the need to base its energy mix on fossil fuels. And in that sense, the development and expansion of renewable energies is key to enabling us to break free from our heavy dependence on oil.

According to estimates by the International Energy Agency (IEA), the growth of sustainable energy sources will tend to increase exponentially in the short and medium term. Among the different forms, they estimate that solar energy will have a significant predominance.

With this in mind, we see that the two areas where renewable energy is expected to play a fundamental role by 2030 are in the generation of electricity and in transportation. In this regard, it can be seen that the IEA expects the increase in green energy in both segments to be more than considerable. In the case of solar energy, it is seen to play a vital role in alternative forms of electricity generation to fossil fuels.

At this point, it's clear to me that focusing on the evolution of electricity generation with different energy sources is key to understanding the company's potential future development. In this sense, and again referring to the IEA, we see that solar energy could play an important role in changing the energy mix over the next five years. While hydroelectric power currently has a predominant impact, its relative weight is expected to decline in the face of greater growth in wind and solar energy, in particular.

Geographically, however, it's important to analyze how different nations position themselves in this scenario. It can be seen that in this regard, the United States and especially China will play a leading role. Both regions, as I will show below, are two places where the company maintains an active stake. This shows that Nextracker is strategically well positioned to confront the major players who will drive this change.

Nextracker Inside Industry

As previously mentioned, the company maintains a strong presence in the American and Asian markets. Considering these as the main regions that could drive solar energy growth, Nextracker, as a service provider for the industry, has an advantage by being present in both geographies.

Now, a valid question might be how Nextracker fits into the solar energy industry. Through its own developments, the company offers solutions for installing solar panels in large fields around the world. Its developments include solar trackers so panel installation can be adapted to the terrain. This facilitates installation in large fields and allows for significant cost reductions in several ways.

First, it avoids the need for a "straight-row" installation, which requires capital expenditures for site maintenance, land relocation, and other expenses to prepare the area for solar panel installation. Thanks to Nextracker technology, this is no longer necessary, allowing for cost savings. Secondly, by adapting to the terrain, solar panel foundations do not require larger piles, which saves on the amount of steel used in their installation. Thirdly, the strategic targeting enabled by Nextracker technology allows for the optimization of captured energy, thus increasing the productivity of the installations where they are used.

This has allowed the company to become a global leader in providing assistance and support for the installation of solar panels in large projects around the world. This allows it to participate in installations generating a total of 100 GigaWatts worldwide. Among the major projects in which the company has participated are the Al Kahfah Photovoltaic Solar Park, located in Saudi Arabia, with a generating capacity of 1.17 GW. Other important projects include Malindi Solar in Kenya, with a generating capacity of 0.52 GW.

Let's now look at the company's revenue growth since its IPO. What we can see is that Nextracker's EBITDA and Net Income have been steadily growing since the company went public. And in the context of solar energy development I described earlier, the likelihood of this growth path continuing is high.

It's important to emphasize that, from my perspective, the world's leading company in this type of technology will have an important role to play in the development, expansion, and efficiency of solar energy generation in the short and medium term.

In the last conference call, Chuck Boynton, the company's CFO, stated that Nextracker expects the following results for the short term.

For the full year fiscal '25, we expect revenue to be in the range of $2.8 billion to $2.9 billion, adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $700 million to $740 million, adjusted diluted EPS to be in the range of $3.75 to $3.95 per share, U.S. revenue mix to be approximately two thirds of the total. We expect our structural gross margins to be in the low 30s, reflecting our continued execution, pricing discipline and cost management.

These statements follow the company's announcements in its latest Shareholder Letter, which revised many of its short-term forecasts upward. As can be seen below, Adjusted EBTIDA, GAAP Net Income, and EPS are up from their expected values ​​for the immediate future.

One of the things I'd like to draw attention to is the company's increase in the number of shares outstanding. While the jump is significant, it's important to recognize that Nextracker is a relatively new company undergoing strong growth. In such a scenario, it's expected that this growth will be funded by issuing shares. However, from my perspective, it's important to monitor this trend to ensure it doesn't continue in the future.

If we look at the company's ratios, as a growing company, we see that the stock is attractive in and of itself. Not only compared to the industry but also in absolute terms. In that sense, the investment thesis for Nextracker is more than interesting. Especially if the growth expected in the short and medium term materializes.

The last aspect I'd like to mention is the capital structure. The company currently maintains a much larger amount of cash in cash relative to its debt stock. It's clear that for a young, growing company, the amount of debt it can take on is limited. It's fair to expect that as the company consolidates, Nextracker's leverage will increase. Even so, the current capital structure, in my view, is sound for the short term, something I rate positively.

Last Quarter Results

I'd now like to briefly summarize the company's latest results. I don't intend to go into extensive detail here, but rather to recap the aspects that I consider most important in understanding how the company fared in the last quarter. In this regard, it is important to note that the December closing date corresponded to 3Q2024, unlike what usually occurs.

When we begin to analyze the results, we see that in the nine months ending in December 2024, the company's revenue amounted to $2.03 billion. This represents an increase of approximately 15.34% compared to the $1.76 billion in the 2023 period. After adjusting for costs, gross profit showed a similar trend, but even more pronounced in terms of growth. Gross profit for these nine months totaled $703 million, compared to $472.5 million in 2023. This represents a 48.78% year-over-year increase.

Regarding Adjusted EBITDA, we see that the quarter ending in December 2024 reached a peak in terms of revenue. The 3Q2024 closed with an Adjusted EBITDA of $186 million. If we take the amount generated in the last year, we see that the total value amounts to $693.6 million. Considering the company's current EV, what we see is that Nextracker's EV/EBITDA ratio is around 7.67x. For a growing company, in my view, this is an attractive multiple. It's true that compared to other companies I've covered, the ratio is high. However, I think it's important to consider that the company is still in a growth phase with very positive prospects, as I tried to show at the beginning of the report. Below we can see the evolution of Adjusted EBITDA over the last few quarters.

Finally, I wanted to mention the FCF the company generated in the nine months corresponding to the 2024 financial year. The total reached $395 million, compared to $314 million in 2023. This represents a 26% increase.

In that sense, while in my personal assessment I prefer companies with a capital-intensive business, it's true that the advantages of companies like Nextracker, where CAPEX investment is relatively low compared to revenue, are undeniable. The main disadvantage of these companies is that they command very high valuations based on EV/EBITDA, PE, or similar multiples. This is not the case with Nextracker, which is why, in my opinion, it is a more than attractive option. Another advantage, as I already mentioned, is the company's very low level of leverage. With cash well above total debt, solvency is not a concern in this case.

Valuation

To further contextualize the company's current situation, I'd like to present a DCF model I developed for the valuation. While it's a subjective analysis, I believe it can help understand how undervalued or undervalued NExtracker may be relative to market value. In that regard, I'll discuss the assumptions I used to develop the model and the results I obtained.

Regarding the company's effective tax rate, I calculated a value of 20%, which is in line with the average of the latest data available in SeekingAlpha's Financials segment. Regarding perpetual growth, and given that Nextracker is still a young company, I proposed a rate of 2.5%. In this regard, I sought to reflect the company's medium- and long-term growth and the expected expansion of the solar energy industry in the face of the energy matrix replacement detailed in the first part of the report.

Regarding the WACC, I used a value of 11%, also to reflect the company's solvency level and its short-term growth.

If we now analyze the projected EBIT, based on the most recently reported figures, I assumed an increase of 8.5% in the first year, 6.5% in the second, 4.5% in the third, and 3.5% in the fourth, ultimately converging to the perpetual growth rate. Regarding amortization and depreciation, given that this is a relatively low amount based on the company's line of business, I kept it practically constant. Finally, regarding CAPEX, I looked for a steady decline. This decline primarily reflects a return to the average seen in the SeekingAlpha data, but it could change if the company decides to significantly increase its size. In any case, it can be seen that its weight is far from representing a highly susceptible value in the model.

With all this in mind, we can see that the company shows upside potential, which, in my opinion, is interesting. Add to that the future projection, and Nextracker is a candidate worth considering.

Risks

Before concluding, I'd like to briefly summarize the potential risks that investors and the company in general could face. Being linked to the energy industry, Nextracker could be strongly affected by two factors that I consider important to highlight.

First, global economic growth is closely linked to the growth in energy demand. In this regard, it is important to mention the new foreign trade policy proposed by Washington. Since Trump's inauguration, the rise in tariffs by the United States and the possibility of a trade war have worried markets. This is largely because the new international trade framework proposed by the United States, if this series of measures intensifies, could lead to a slowdown in global GDP growth or even a recession.

Finally, the speed of change in the global energy mix will depend largely on the evolution of fossil fuel prices. As the international price of crude oil falls, the need to replace it with alternative energy sources loses its appeal. As long as oil is abundant and cheap, modifying the mix with another form of green energy does not appear to gain traction. On the contrary, as oil prices increase, the need to replace it would gain momentum.

Conclusion

In conclusion, we saw that solar energy maintains an important role in the expected shift from conventional to more sustainable energies. According to IEA estimates, the growth of solar energy as a means of generating electricity is expected to increase in the medium and long term and maintain an exponential trend, as is the case with the total use of green energy. In this sense, a company like Nextracker plays a more than important role. Furthermore, we saw that the company maintains an active role in countries expected to lead these changes and this increase in supply. Furthermore, if we analyze the company's ratios, we see that the company maintains attractive values. In this sense, the company's solvency is also a value worth highlighting. Finally, the DCF model provides upside potential that seems to partially justify this thesis. For all these reasons, I consider Nextracker a good buy opportunity.

data.title: Theratechnologies: A High-Risk, High-Reward Speculation As Shares Trade At 39% Discount To Future Pak's $3.51-$4.50 Offer
data.description:
What Happened

Theratechnologies’ (NASDAQ:THTX) shares trade at about $2.52 per share as of April 18, 2025, despite the fact that Future Pak publicly outlined on April 11, 2025 an unsolicited proposal valuing the company at $3.51 to $4.50 per share—equating to up to $255 million in enterprise value, or a 39%–78% premium to current levels.

Future Pak first approached Theratechnologies in August 2024 with a non‑binding $100 million cash proposal, which the board deemed insufficient. After a second overture in January 2025 stalled due to exclusivity with another suitor, Future Pak on April 11 reiterated its offer of $205 million of cash plus up to $50 million of contingent value rights tied to EGRIFTA SV performance—totaling $255 million in enterprise value .

At this point, we think Theratechnologies is taking selling the company much more seriously. On April 16, it announced it will further explore selling the company utilizing a special committee to "make a recommendation to the full board." It has also sought external advisors to facilitate this process. For reasons discussed below, we think the recommendation will be to take the Future Pak offer, assuming there is not a better offer out there and Futuer Pak's offer remains on the table by the time this process is completed.

Pipeline & Commercial Franchise

Theratechnologies’ HIV franchise is anchored by EGRIFTA SV, which in Q1 2025 recorded $13.9 million in net sales. Trogarzo suffered a 22.4% decline to $5.2 million amid intensifying competition.

On March 25, the FDA approved a supplemental BLA for EGRIFTA WR, a once‑weekly formulation intended to improve adherence. The company plans to commence a commercial launch in July 2025. However, we are uncertain whether this will turn things around for the company, as EGRIFTA sales have been largely underwhelming to date.

Indeed, this appears to have been largely attributed to temporary supply disruptions earlier this year, which, according to the company, caused a "sales loss of six to seven weeks." However, even with that loss, sales going forward would need to increase substantially to make an impact. We see the company's exploration of strategic alternatives as an indication that they may not see that happening in a short enough timeframe to sustain business operations without a large external capital injection.

Outside of HIV, Tryngolza (olezarsen) received full FDA approval on December 19, 2024, for familial chylomicronemia syndrome—a disorder affecting fewer than 5,000 U.S. patients. This is a product it licensed from Ionis (IONS), which gives it exclusive rights to market the product in Canada. Donidalorsen awaits an August 21, 2025 PDUFA decision for hereditary angioedema, though any approval would likely add only incremental revenue in a niche market.

Financial Overview & Cash Runway

In our view, a quick glance over the company's financials underscores that time is of the essence for the company to sell itself. At quarter‑end, the company held just $4.6 million in cash, down from $19.8 million at fiscal‑year end, and burned $9.74 million of operating cash flow during Q1 2025.

We estimate that, at the current burn rate, Theratechnologies has less than one quarter of runway (potentially only 2 months), unless it secures external financing or a strategic transaction. In our view, this rapidly dwindling liquidity makes it likely that, unless a buyer emerges very soon, shareholders should expect a dilutive equity or debt raise in the next month.

Risks

We caution that Future Pak’s proposals remain non‑binding and could be withdrawn if the board declines to engage or due diligence uncovers issues . Theratechnologies’ exclusivity discussions with another suitor could yield an alternate transaction on different terms. Moreover, up to $50 million of total consideration hinges on EGRIFTA SV performance targets; failure to meet those would materially reduce proceeds. Finally, the company’s limited cash runway and likely need for dilutive financing add execution and timing risk.

Our Takeaway

In our opinion, the company will soon accept the Future Pak offer. Thus, we think the 39%–78% spread between the $2.52 trading price and Future Pak’s $3.51–$4.50 offer presents an appealing event‑driven speculation for investors willing to assume significant risk. We see it as possible Future Pak could even raise its bid during negotiations with the company, but we do not think it will be that much higher given the financial stress that the company is currently under, in our opinion.

The modest scale of the HIV franchise revenues, small absolute uptake of Tryngolza, and limited upside from EGRIFTA WR temper downside protection, in our opinion, while a completed transaction would deliver substantial upside. That said, the rapid cash burn, non‑binding nature of the bids, and the prospect of near‑term dilution impose meaningful timing pressure.

data.title: Private Real Estate And The Impact Of Tariffs: Stay Measured
data.description:
By Chase Bolding, Chief Investment Officer, Direct Real Estate, North America, & Mike Sobolik, Investment Strategist, Direct Real Estate, North America

Sharp shifts in US tariff policies have caused disruption to capital markets. We’ve produced this opinion primarily with U.S. market conditions in mind, but we acknowledge this is a global situation, extending well beyond the scope of this memo.

  1. Stay measured. Don’t swing hard in either direction until there’s more clarity.Invesco Real Estate is taking a measured approach to tariff-induced market volatility. While continuous trading in the public markets can drive hyper-responsiveness, we believe private markets have the benefit of digesting information more deliberately, without emotional spikes.
  2. Growth concerns call for a review of existing and new investments.We believe that risks around economic growth and inflation have increased. At a minimum, uncertainty will cloud decision-making for some tenants in the near term. This shift in conditions warrants a review of risk/reward within portfolios and new investment opportunities.
  3. Transaction volume could decline as investors, notably foreign capital, move to the sidelines and debt availability becomes more selective.It’s too early to know if volumes will diminish materially this year because of tariffs. There’s a positive correlation between transaction volume and asset price growth. Thus, reduced activity could dampen appreciation, all else being equal.
  4. It’s not all negative, but sentiment and growth are the dominating factors right now.The increased risk environment could lead to lower interest rates and further reductions of new supply. We acknowledge these positives, but, at present, we’re more focused on demand, credit quality of tenants, and capital flows.
  5. Historically, periods of lower liquidity have presented attractive investment windows.We know from experience that attractive entry points emerge during periods of disruption and volatility when there is less competition. As investors digest tariff implications, we’ll be looking for those opportunities.
  6. Real estate credit still looks attractive.Private credit provides structural protections that can be attractive if the impact of tariffs lead to weaker economic conditions.

Important information

NA4400739

Image: AerialPerspective Images / Getty

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

Investments in real estate-related instruments may be affected by economic, legal, or environmental factors that affect property values, rents or occupancies of real estate. Real estate companies, including REITs or similar structures, tend to be small and mid-cap companies and their shares may be more volatile and less liquid.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of March 25, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

data.title: Monster Beverage: Continue To Expect Growth Acceleration
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage of Monster Beverage (NASDAQ:MNST) in January 2024, in which I recommended a buy rating due to my expectation that growth should accelerate, supported by better underlying demand, pricing, and comp base, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. Based on the latest developments: stronger channel trends, shelf space expansion, and better pricing, I believe MNST remains well-positioned for continued growth acceleration. I reiterate my buy rating.

Investment thesis

I continue to believe MNST is in a good position to continue delivering stronger growth over the next several quarters.

To start, MNST implemented a ~5% U.S. price increase across its core brands in November 2024 (per the 4Q24 earnings call), and retailer surveys (credits to Goldman Sachs) conducted after the price increase showed that 80% of outlets believe the pricing move is sticking. This suggests two things: (1) MNST pricing power remains intact, and (2) FY25 should at least see 5% growth, which itself is half of FY24 growth (FY24 grew ~10% y/y), as the full benefit of this price increase should start flowing more cleanly into results beginning in 1Q25.

Additionally, channel data trends (credits to Morgan Stanley) are supportive of growth acceleration in FY25. Although MNST experienced some softness in January due to wildfires and severe weather in California, growth has since bounced back to high-single-digit, and remember that 1Q24 (~12% y/y revenue growth) was a tough comp base relative to 2-4Q24 (average ~3% y/y revenue growth). Assuming the channel data is representative of actual revenue growth, with 1Q25 growth accelerating to high-single-digit (against a tough base), the remaining quarters should see even better y/y growth rates (an acceleration).

Lastly, I also believe the potential gains in shelf and cooler space during the spring resets should serve as an important catalyst for MNST growth reacceleration. According to Goldman Sachs’ January 2025 retailer survey, MNST is expected to gain ~1.5% shelf and cooler space on a weighted average store basis during the upcoming resets. While 1.5% may not seem like a lot, in the beverage industry, this modest improvement in shelf placement can have outsized impacts. Specifically, it improves consumer visibility, which potentially leads to higher purchase frequency, and this is especially true for categories like energy drinks, where consumers focus on brand loyalty and make impulse purchases.

Moreover, this shelf space expansion comes along with MNST rolling out new products, which have received very good adoption from consumers. Take the Monster Ultra Vice Guava, for instance. Per the data compiled by Goldman Sachs, customer and consumer response has been overwhelmingly positive, with 91% of surveyed retailers indicating the launch has been either "moderately strong" or "very strong."

In my view, this combination of shelf space expansion and successful innovation should help MNST gain market share through 2025, which helps in accelerating growth.

Why the Spruce Point Short Report Fails to Change the Bullish Thesis

Turning to the Spruce Point short report, I am not fully convinced about the bear case put forth. Concerns around the alcohol brands segment, for example, seem overblown. MNST did record a $130.7 million impairment charge tied to its Alcohol Brands division in 4Q24, but management had already been transparent about the challenges facing this business since mid-2024. More importantly, alcohol represents only ~2% of total company revenue, meaning that any impact is extremely minimal on a consolidated basis.

Next, the idea that MNST pricing power is eroding is just not true, in my view. As I noted above, not only did MNST push through a ~5% price increase in November 2024, but retailer surveys confirm that the majority of outlets are seeing that price increase stick. Furthermore, gross margin expansion in 4Q24 (100 bps expansion) clearly shows that MNST is successfully offsetting input costs, which shows that the increase in prices (organic and through mix) is more than raising input costs. The concerns around aluminum tariffs are also not as bad as they seem. MNST has already hedged a significant portion of its aluminum exposure for 2025, as management explicitly stated. This limits downside risk even if spot prices remain volatile.

However, we are hedged to quite a nice extent in 2025 with aluminum, and we have some hedges on the Midwest premium. 4Q24 earnings results call

As for competition from Celsius and Alani Nu, I see merits to the argument, but as of the data, I am seeing (referring to the shelf space expansion chart), it is not as if MNST is the loser here. MNST is still growing shelf space. The losers (losing market share) are Zoa, Vita Coco, Keurig Dr Pepper Inc., and Zevia. MNST’s new products are also seeing strong demand, and it is a fact that growth has reaccelerated per the channel checks data.

Valuation

I get the sense that the market is leaning toward my bullish view that the short report is not as concerning as it seems to be, since then, the share price has largely recovered back to near the pre-report release level. At this price, I still think there is an upside to the stock. My growth assumptions are still the same. Using the recent channel data as a benchmark, growth should trend in the high single digits in FY25, followed by an acceleration to 10% as MNST gains more shelf space and rolls out more new products. MNST has also shown that it can expand margins in 4Q24, so I expect that momentum to continue. Even without multiple expansions, I see a 14% upside to the stock. One can argue that multiples should go higher since both growth and margin profiles have improved, and if true, the upside is even better.

Risk

The risks to my bullish thesis are (1) if there is slower than expected consumer uptake of new product innovations. While the recent data is positive, this does not mean every product will be welcomed; (2) margin pressure if commodity costs rise beyond current hedge levels; and (3) MNST started to lose shelf space to emerging brands like Celsius for whatever reason. This will hit the stock’s sentiment badly, even if it doesn’t immediately impact MNST growth.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for MNST is still a buy. I continue to believe MNST has strong underlying growth momentum, with multiple visible growth drivers through FY25, including favorable pricing, shelf expansion, and new product success. In my view, the short report by Spruce Point is not a big concern, and there is still upside potential in the stock price.

data.title: Smithfield Foods: Not Tasty In This Environment
data.description:
One company that just became publicly traded once again earlier this year is Smithfield Foods (NASDAQ:SFD). The specific date was January 28th of this year. This is the first time that the stock has become available for investors since the business was acquired in 2013 by Shuanghui International Holdings in a deal valued at $7.1 billion. Given that I'm always on the lookout for attractive opportunities, and considering how much of a foodie I am, I couldn't resist digging into this opportunity. What I found was a business that is trading on the cheap. However, beneath the hood, financial performance looks disappointing. Revenue has been falling and the bottom-line results of the business have been all over the map. As much as I want to be bullish about the company, I think it is premature to do so. Because of this, I have no choice but to rate it a ‘hold’ at this time.

Taking a bite out of Smithfield Foods

Founded way back in 1936, Smithfield Foods is most certainly an old business. It's also a firm that my family and I have bought from for as long as I can remember. It is a multinational brand that employs 34,000 people in the US and 2,500 in Mexico. The firm's portfolio includes a number of high-quality iconic brands, including its flagship brand Smithfield, Eckrich, and Nathan’s Famous.

According to management, the company holds the number 2 market position by volume across 25 different categories. These specific categories include packaged meats. Last year, the $14.14 billion in revenue that the business generated represented a sizable chunk of what management considers to be the $47 billion value-added packaged meats market in the US. After being taken private back in 2013, the enterprise began transforming its operations. This culminated with its decision to carve out its European operations and to focus its efforts on the North American market.

The firm's transformation has involved a significant scaling back of the specific products that it offers. Since 2019, the firm has reduced its packaged meat SKU count by over 40%. But this doesn't mean that the enterprise has been focused on shrinking. Instead, it has focused on growing rapidly in the categories that it has remained dedicated to. Over the same window of time, volume velocity as measured by pounds per item grew by over 60%. In the company's efforts to change its operations around, the acquirers of the business allocated $3 billion toward capital spending projects.

This had a generally positive effect on the firm. Its overall revenue expanded from $13.4 billion in 2014 to $14.6 billion in 2023. For the packaged meat category, revenue popped from $7.2 billion to $8.3 billion. The company did all of this while more than doubling its segment profit margin from 6% to 13%, bringing its overall segment profits up from $460 million to $1.07 billion.

When it comes to the firm's packaged meat operations, it's important to note that, in 2023, the business sold about 2.9 billion pounds worth. 63% of the sales come from retail channels, with 31% coming from the foodservice category and the remaining 6% involving industrial channels. The business provides its goods to all of the top 10 ranked national grocery retailers and to 70% of the top 50 ranked national foodservice chains. It counts all of the top foodservice distributors such as Sysco (SYY) and Performance Food Group (PFGC) as its customers.

The company also focuses on the pork space. If management is to be believed, the firm is the largest fresh pork processor in the US. Through these operations, it processed around 29 million heads of product in 2023. About a third of these fresh pork products are sold to its own packaged meat operations. This creates a feeder structure, quite literally, allowing the packaged meat business to get around 80% of its raw materials without having to go to any outside parties. Through its own facilities, with locations based throughout the East Coast, the Midwest, and states such as Missouri and Utah, the company provides itself with around half of the raw materials associated with its fresh pork business. This level of integration should, in theory, permit significant cost savings that have the potential to create additional value for investors as time goes on.

Unfortunately, the financial performance of the business has been less than stellar recently. From 2022 to 2024, revenue for the company contracted from $16.20 billion to $14.14 billion. To truly understand this decline, we should look at each of the three core operating segments of the business. During the three-year window covered, the business saw its Packaged Meats segment report a decline in revenue from $9.26 billion to $8.32 billion. All of this decline, and more, came between 2022 and 2023, when revenue plummeted to only $8.28 billion. That drop of 10.6% was driven by a 7.2% fall in volume that management claimed was the result of soft consumer demand. Some of the decline, 2.7%, was because of the firm's decision to sell off one of its operations called Saratoga in a deal that closed in October 2022 and that brought in proceeds of $575 million. The company also saw average sales prices drop during this time by 3.7% because of the soft demand environment.

In 2024, revenue for this segment expanded slightly to $8.32 billion. This increase of $39 million was thanks to a 3.1% rise in the average sales price of the company's products. Management claimed that this was because of higher raw material costs that the business had to pass on to its customers. However, this increase was largely offset by a 2.5% drop in volume caused mostly by lower bacon sales that management blamed on group housing legislation in California and Massachusetts that required pork producers across the US to change their production standards if they desired to continue operating in those states. Lower holiday ham sales, presumably because of softer consumer demand, also proved problematic.

This wasn't the only part of the company that saw weakness. From 2022 to 2024, the company’s Fresh Pork segment saw revenue drop from $9.19 billion to $7.87 billion. This fall all occurred from 2022 to 2023, with revenue plummeting 14.8% because of lower fresh pork market prices that impacted sales negatively to the tune of 10.5% and because of a reduction in volume of 4.8%. The lower pricing, management said, was the result of soft consumer demand. The decrease in sales volume of 4.8% can be attributed to the company's decision to close its Vernon, California, facility in early 2023.

Lastly, we have the Hog Production segment. Revenue dropped over the last three years from $4.46 billion to $3 billion. Once again, most of this pain came from 2022 to 2023, with revenue plunging 25.6% because of a 16.5% drop in average hog sale prices and because of an 8.5% drop in the number of hogs sold. About $193 million of the sales decline can be attributed to lower grain sales that the company made to other customers. All of this seems to be related to the firm's decision to exit its West Coast business and to make certain changes at its hog production facilities in order to improve its cost structure. One such example here seems to be the firm's decision in 2024 to shut down its ham-boning facility in Altoona, Iowa.

Even though revenue dropped quite a bit over the last couple of years, the bottom line for the business has been more mixed. Net income went from $800 million in 2022 to a negative $138 million. But then, in 2024, profits rebounded to $783 million. Over the last three years, operating cash flow for the company shot up from $521 million to $916 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a slightly more modest rise from $787 million to $1.15 billion. Finally, we have EBITDA. Over the three-year window, we saw a slight increase from $1.20 billion to $1.38 billion. But these results served as bookends for a plunge to $610 million in 2023. We don't really know what to expect when it comes to 2025. But we do know that management expects revenue to grow somewhere between the low to mid-single-digit range.

In its IPO, the company should have received proceeds of about $249 million. This would mean a net debt of $809.9 million. This works out when adding the non-controlling interests for the company and its market capitalization, to an enterprise value of $8.69 billion. Using these estimates, and historical results for the last two completed fiscal years, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. Using the 2024 figures, shares actually do look attractive. The company is also attractively priced compared to other similar enterprises. In the table below, I stacked it up against five similar companies. On a price-to-operating cash flow basis, I found that one of the five companies was cheaper than it is. And when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, we can see that it was the cheapest of the group.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Smithfield Foods 6.7 6.3
Lamb Weston (LW) 9.8 10.8
Post Holdings (POST) 7.3 9.6
Tyson Foods (TSN) 9.3 8.9
Pilgrim's Pride (PPC) 6.3 6.7
Hormel Foods (HRL) 14.2 13.9


It is worth noting that issues for the company could soon get worse. This is because of the trade war that the US started earlier this year. In March, China, which accounts for around 14% of the export volume of US beef, decided not to renew export registrations for the commodity, opting instead to get their beef supply from Australia. The Packaged Meats part of Smithfield Foods does have some exposure to beef. However, it is worth noting that, last year, China purchased $1.38 billion of pork from the US as well. That made it the second-largest foreign buyer of the commodity, accounting for 16% of all pork exports. They have not decided to stop the purchase of American pork. In fact, they ended up renewing those export registrations. However, because of retaliatory tariffs, the effective tariff rate for pork coming from the US to China is around 172%. This will certainly hurt any business in this industry.

If these issues can die down, the picture for the business should improve. This is because, long-term, management expects growth in this space. They believe that from 2023 through 2028, the US value-added packaged meat market will grow at an annualized rate of 3.6%. That would take it from $46.8 billion to $55.8 billion. As a clear leader in the space, Smithfield Foods would benefit from this.

Takeaway

Based on valuation alone, Smithfield Foods looks like an attractive opportunity. But one thing that I value heavily when analyzing any firm is stability. Unfortunately, the last few years have been quite a wild ride from a fundamental perspective. I know that the food industry can be subjected to these kinds of extremes. I have followed the space enough to understand that. But given the significant weaknesses that the business has had in some respects, I believe that a more appropriate rating for the company at this time is a ‘hold’.

data.title: Payoneer: Solid Moat With Strong Industry Tailwinds
data.description:
Summary

I am positive on Payoneer (NASDAQ:PAYO). My view is that PAYO has built a strong moat—its global payment infrastructure—that subscale players will struggle to replicate. So long as it doesn’t mis-execute, it should continue to be in a good position to capitalize on the secular tailwinds. Given the current share price, I see an attractive risk-reward setup if PAYO can simply deliver moderate growth and margin consistency over the next few years.

Company Overview

PAYO is a fintech platform that simplifies cross-border transactions for small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs). Some of the solutions offered include accounts receivable and accounts payable solutions, multi-currency accounts, and working capital access. So far, as of 4Q24, PAYO has ~2 million customers across 190 countries, of which there are ~560k active ideal customer profiles (ICP). The value proposition that PAYO offers is that it enables SMBs to streamline their global financial operations within a single platform.

Scale Advantage and Industry Tailwinds

PAYO has a solid global payment infrastructure that subscale players cannot compete with. PAYO connects to ~100 banking and payment service providers and facilitates transactions across >7,000 trade corridors, supporting real-time settlements in >150 countries (per the latest 10K). This is not an easy feat. To achieve this scale, one has to invest significant time in building relationships with counterparties, incur regulatory and compliance costs, and establish a strong reputation over many years. A strong reputation is important because banks (who are the main counterparty) are generally selective when working with shady partners. As such, replicating PAYO’s scale is extremely difficult and unlikely within a short period of time. This gives PAYO a huge edge to win market share while new entrants or sub-scale players are working their way to scale up. Scale is important because it offers better transaction speed, reliability, and geographic coverage, which are particularly important when serving SMBs that operate internationally.

With this strong edge, I believe PAYO is in a good position to continue winning share and growing in this space. There are a few secular tailwinds. Firstly, the globalization of SMB commerce should continue to accelerate. This is already an ongoing trend per the PAYO SMB ambitions barometer, and if this happens as I expect, there will be a substantial increase in SMBs need to offer relevant solutions like localized payment, multi-currency management, and cross-border disbursement solutions. The exact total addressable market (TAM) is hard to size, but per PAYO’s presentation deck, the TAM is worth as much as $450 billion.

The second tailwind is the digitization of B2B payments, and this is where the real money is. The estimated size is about $6 trillion, and we know PAYO is capturing a lot of demand in this space as B2B payment volumes grew ~42% y/y in FY24. I don’t see a world where businesses will fall back to the legacy way of doing things. The traditional way of doing things is simply too inefficient, costly, slow, and manual. Imagine you are a small business owner in the US trying to pay your supplier in India. In the old way of doing things, you would first write a check, mail it overseas, wait for it to arrive (which could take weeks), have the supplier deposit it into their bank, and then wait even longer for the funds to clear. Each step costs money, such as courier fees, bank processing fees, currency conversion fees, etc., and it wastes time. Plus, if the check gets lost, you have to start over. With digital B2B payments, you simply click a few buttons online. The payment is sent instantly through a secure network, settles much faster, and both you and your supplier can track it in real-time. It’s cheaper, faster, and reduces the chances of anything going wrong.

The last tailwind is the rise of direct-to-consumer (DTC) selling channels. As SMBs increasingly build their own webstores and bypass traditional marketplaces, they will need global, multi-currency checkout solutions, and the PAYO Checkout product addresses this need. Demand is evidently strong. PAYO merchant services volume grew ~147% y/y in FY24 on top of ~473% y/y in FY23. More importantly, this business has much higher take rates, at ~3.9% vs. the other segments with much lower rates. This means, as this scales, it can help accelerate the overall growth of the business.

Valuation

The beauty about investing in PAYO today is that the share price has been beaten down so badly—most likely due to the market sell-down—the upside is very attractive if PAYO is able to just accelerate growth back to 10% and maintain margins at 12%. Both are not aggressive assumptions, in my view. The business was growing way stronger in past years, and I don’t see a significant change to the industry tailwinds. PAYO already did a 12% margin in FY24, so my margin estimate is not a high bar. Moreover, as PAYO scales, margins should expand, which I did not incorporate into my model. My model also assumes PAYO to trade at 22x forward PE. Again, I think this is not too aggressive an assumption since Paycom Software (PAYC) trades at 24x with a growth expectation of 10%.

Investment Risk

As much as the secular tailwinds are strong, PAYO's main customers—SMBs—are still at the mercy of the macro environment. In a downturn or recession, they get hit the most, and this directly impacts PAYO as there will likely be lesser transaction volumes. Additionally, potential competition from traditional banks (they have the infrastructure to do so) could hurt PAYO take rates over time.

Conclusion

I am positive on PAYO because I believe the business is fundamentally strong, supported by a solid moat and secular trends. Valuation is also not expensive at the current share price level. For an investor to yield an attractive upside, PAYO simply needs to show modest growth acceleration and sustain margins at the current level.

data.title: UGI: This Dividend Aristocrat Is Outperforming And Undervalued
data.description:
Dividend Aristocrats are outperforming so far in 2025. The ProShares S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (NOBL) is down by just 3.4% year to date, posting more than 6 percentage points of alpha to the S&P 500.

Today, I’m revisiting UGI Corporation, a steady eddy Gas Utilities industry company within the relatively strong Utilities sector. It has increased its annual payout since the early 1970s, with a $1.50 cumulative dividend paid in CY 2025. After a solid earnings report in February, I reiterate a buy rating and I am raising my price target.

Shares are up a whopping 35% in total return since my Q3 2024 analysis. I see a bit more upside, though the gap between the stock price and intrinsic value has shrunk. I’ll note key spots on the chart to monitor ahead of the Q1 report due out next month.

UGI Dividend History

NOBL ETF Beating SPY YTD

Utilities Outperforming in 2025

Back in February, UGI reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $1.37 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $1.19, while revenue of $2.0 billion, down 4% from the same period a year earlier, was a $620 million miss. Adjusted EPS rose 14% year-over-year, driven by solid performance in its natural gas and international propane segments. Despite the top-line miss, shares rose 2% in the following session, and the options market prices in a small 4.3% stock price swing after the May 7 reporting date.

Looking closer at the previous quarter, the management team voiced optimism and maintained its full-year guidance for 2025. It will be interesting to see if the same sanguine stance is echoed on the upcoming call in light of the more precarious macroeconomic backdrop. Of course, UGI operates in a non-cyclical area, which is perhaps part of the reason for its sharp outperformance in recent months.

Still, the firm reported some operational challenges as it focuses on operational improvements, but UGI still sees EPS to verify in the range of $2.75 to $3.05 this year. It plans to leverage its international assets for growth, though both domestic and ex-US markets could be volatile for the balance of 2025.

I like that the management team is open to divesting underperforming assets to improve liquidity and reduce debt, and we could see additional EPS gains from its AmeriGas operation. As it stands, liquidity is $1.5 billion, and UGI filed for rate increases in Pennsylvania with plans to redeem AmeriGas Senior Notes.

On the earnings outlook, it's steady as she goes. We could see a slight EPS giveback in 2025, but out-year EPS growth is expected. Revenue gains are seen as solid over the next handful of quarters, and profitability trends are impressive.

Along with returning considerable capital to shareholders (the dividend yield is now 4.57% as of April 16, 2025), free cash flow per share is $1.77. Finally, the sellside is upbeat about UGI—there have been three analyst upgrades compared with zero downgrades in the last 90 days.

UGI: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have just minor adjustments versus last September. I remain positive and expect forward 12-month non-GAAP EPS to come in near $3.05. I am willing to raise my multiple assumptions from 10x to 11.5x based on rising profits and a market-wide shift toward less cyclical industries, which should persist, thereby supporting names like UGI.

11.5x is still below the stock’s long-term average P/E, so that could be conservative. The resulting valuation is $35, making this high-yield dividend grower still undervalued. UGI also trades inexpensively on a price-to-sales basis.

UGI: Favorable Valuation Metrics

Key risks for UGI include some operational challenges that were discussed on the earnings call, but they did not appear to be very burdensome. Higher costs of goods sold and rising competition are potential threats to earnings, while the executives should be prudent about asset sales so as not to negatively affect EPS.

Of course, higher interest rates could adversely affect the firm given the debt load. Finally, unfavorable rate case outcomes would be a headwind.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon shows a confirmed Q2 2025 earnings date of Wednesday, May 7 AMC with a conference call immediately after the numbers cross the wires. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

I was bullish on UGI last fall before the breakout, but was admittedly cautious on the chart. Then came the huge upside advance in late November. The stock soared from $24 to $30 in the wake of its Q4 quarterly report. A pullback to $27 transpired, but the bulls retook the reins, sending shares to a multi-year high above $34.

The recent retreat to $29 proved to be a buying opportunity. Notice in the chart below that the $34-$36 range could be a tough zone. The good news is that the long-term 200-day moving average is positively sloped, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also, take a look at the RSI momentum gauge at the top of the graph—it touched technical oversold conditions earlier this month during the macro tariff selloff, and that resolved a bearish RSI/price divergence. UGI has just about filled the downside gap from a few weeks ago, so the onus is on the bulls to bring the stock through resistance. Support is near $29, with long-term demand likely to come into play at $26.

UGI: Bullish Breakout Last Year, Mid-$30s Resistance, Rising Long-Term 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on UGI. It’s a reliable dividend payer with a modest P/E. The chart broke out last year, and while resistance is in play, the broader technical trend is higher ahead of the Q1 report due out in May.

data.title: Midstream Fundamentals Vs. Tariffs And Market Turmoil
data.description:
By Brian Watson, Senior Portfolio Manager

The equity markets, including energy equities, have experienced sharp declines over recent days as investors grapple with the uncertainties sparked by the rollout of U.S. tariffs. Midstream equities have not been immune, giving back recent gains but still outperforming the broader markets. In fact, through Friday, April 4, 2025, the Alerian MLP Index is down only 1.4% year-to-date on a price basis and up 0.3% once distributions are considered versus the S&P 500 Index’s 13.7% price loss and 13.4% total return loss.1

Natural gas demand growth appears resilient

Importantly, midstream companies earning a majority of their margin from natural gas-centric services account for roughly 75% of midstream sector market capitalization. (e.g., gathering, transportation, processing, fractionation, NGL logistics).2 Accordingly, in our view, midstream sector participants remain well-positioned to benefit from the robust outlook for U.S. natural gas demand which we believe will see little impact from U.S. tariffs or heightened trade barriers enacted in retaliation.

Domestic natural gas demand is positioned to benefit from the start-up of multiple liquified natural gas (LNG) export facilities, all underwritten by long-term supply contracts with limited exposure to tariffs. Natural gas demand is also expected to benefit from rising demand for natural gas-fueled electric generation to meet the needs of data centers and the broader trend toward “electrification of everything” including transportation, heating, and industrial processes.

Further, any incremental onshoring of manufacturing that is achieved, which appears to be a key tenet of President Trump’s underlying objectives, only adds to domestic power and natural gas demand. The administration has also often touted the contracting of additional U.S.-sourced LNG as a negotiating vehicle.

Healthy outlook remains

Therefore, despite the market turmoil currently unfolding, we believe the midstream sector offers investors an attractive distribution yield and an improving outlook for cash flow and distribution growth. In our view, these fundamentals are supported by significant and predictable natural gas volume growth that may near 25-34% by the end of the decade.3 Further, midstream sector leverage and distribution coverage metrics remain healthy, which we believe insulates sector growth plans from market volatility.

Footnotes

1 Bloomberg L.P. as of April 4, 2025.

2 Bloomberg as of 2/28/25

3 Bernstein, The Long View: A US gas supercycleis coming... we upgrade gassy E&Ps, 01/15/25 and KinderMorgan1Q25 Investor Presentation, 2/28/2025

____________

NA4411057

The Alerian MLP Index is a capped, float-adjusted, capitalization-weighted index, whose constituents earn the majority oftheir cash flow from midstream activities involving energy commodities.

An investment cannot be made into an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions, there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

Most MLPs operate in the energy sector and are subject to the risks generally applicable to companies in that sector, including commodity pricing risk, supply and demand risk, depletion risk and exploration risk. MLPs are also subject the risk that regulatory or legislative changes could eliminate the tax benefits enjoyed by MLPs which could have a negative impact on the after-tax income available for distribution by the MLPs and/or the value of the portfolio’s investments. Although the characteristics of MLPs closely resemble a traditional limited partnership, a major difference is that MLPs may trade on a public exchange or in the over-the-counter market. Although this provides a certain amount of liquidity, MLP interests may be less liquid and subject to more abrupt or erratic price movements than conventional publicly traded securities. The risks of investing in an MLP are similar to those of investing in a partnership and include more flexible governance structures, which could result in less protection for investors than investments in a corporation. MLPs are generally considered interest-rate sensitive investments. During periods of interest rate volatility, these investments may not provide attractive returns.

Energy infrastructure MLPs are subject to a variety of industry specific risk factors that may adversely affect their business or operations, including those due to commodity production, volumes, commodity prices, weather conditions, terrorist attacks, etc. They are also subject to significant federal, state and local government regulation.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of April 17, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. The opinions are based on current market conditions and are subject to change. They may differ from these of other Invesco investment professionals.

Midstream fundamentals vs tariffs and market turmoil by Invesco US

data.title: How Likely Is It That The Bottom Has Not Yet Been Reached?
data.description:
The tariff pause seems to have restored a glimmer of confidence to the markets; at least we are no longer experiencing double-digit declines in 48 hours. However, the underlying problem remains and Trump does not seem willing to change his modus operandi. I believe that the purpose of the tariffs is simply to negotiate more favorable trade agreements, but his statements may not be conducive to achieving this goal:

I'm telling you, these countries are calling us up, kissing my ass. They are. They are dying to make a deal.

I'm not sure this is the best way to negotiate, but who am I to judge. The market also still has many doubts and volatility is very high, so everything can change very quickly. No one knows if the bottom has already been reached, but we can look to the past for some insight into the future. Every crisis has different triggers, but there are similarities that can help us.

Death Cross and Yield Curve

In recent days, there has been much talk about the S&P 500 experiencing a death cross after several years. For those who don't know, a death cross occurs when the 50-day moving average breaks below the 200-day moving average.

Typically, this signal is interpreted as a potential continuation of the downward trend. But is it really that reliable? Perhaps not, and I'll explain why.

To begin with, I'm not a big fan of technical analysis, so I wouldn't base an investment thesis solely on looking at a chart. However, there are some signals that are actually more useful than others, but I don't think the death cross is one of the significant ones. There are two main reasons for this: it's not statistically significant and it's often lagging.

Throughout history, there have been many more death crosses than it might seem when considering only the last few years. In 50 years, the S&P 500 has recorded 24 death crosses, and obviously not all of them predicted a recession. In particular, only in 46% of cases did the index fall further after the death cross signal. If anything, given that the chances of the index rising after the death cross are higher than those of a further decline, this indicator is more bullish than bearish. In 100 years of data, in the 20 days following the death cross, the S&P 500 fell 52% of the time: almost the same predictive power as a coin toss.

The death cross is a lagging signal, so it does not tell you if the bottom has been reached, only that the trend has reversed based on the data obtained up to that point. The point is that we do not want to know what has happened, but what will happen. If you wait for the golden cross (the opposite of the death cross) before investing, you risk leaving a large portion of your gains on the table. Similarly, if you start selling everything when there is a death cross, you risk selling right near the bottom.

I don't understand why people give so much importance to the death cross; it's probably because of its dramatic name. When something is called a “death cross,” you expect the worst, but this is almost never the case. Only on three occasions has the death cross lived up to its name: 1981, 2000, and 2007. In those cases, the S&P 500 lost 21%, 45%, and 55% respectively after the signal.

If you think about it, it is no coincidence that the death cross proved reliable in 2000 and 2007, as both were serious crises that lasted 1-2 years. When there is a flash crash like in 2020, being a lagging indicator is not very helpful, because by the time it occurs, the bottom has already been reached. From this point of view, there may be similarities with what we are experiencing today: a rapid crisis that could be resolved quickly if tariffs were no longer the topic of the day.

Overall, I would not consider the death cross as a signal that tells us whether the bottom has been reached. A different case is the spread between 10-year and 2-year government bonds.

Many believe that the sign of a recession is when the yield on 2-year government bonds exceeds that on 10-year bonds, resulting in a downward sloping yield curve. In reality, this is only the first trigger, but the recession occurs when the yield curve re-inverts and the 10-year rate exceeds the 2-year rate again. So technically there was no false signal, simply the first inversion lasted much longer than expected due to out-of-control inflation. Now the re-inversion process is underway, we have well exceeded the 0 threshold, and from here on the pitfalls begin. It typically takes a few months/quarters for a recession to occur after the re-inversion, and we are exactly at that point. While two months ago I could not have imagined what the trigger for the recession would be, today I have some ideas. But I want to make one thing clear.

It is not certain that we will have a recession in the coming months/quarters; no indicator can show this with certainty. However, the yield curve has proven to be reliable since the 1990s, and frankly, I cannot tell you why today should be any different. In the event of a recession, unemployment will rise, household consumption will fall, and a lower bottom than that reached in early April will be inevitable.

The resilience of American households

After causing panic with my yield curve speech, I now want to show you something positive. There are never only negative or positive signs, and it is right to look at the other side of the coin.

First of all, it is important to understand that recession is an inevitable phase of the economic cycle and cannot be avoided, so the sooner you accept this, the better. Trying to predict it and shorting the market will not make you rich in most cases; the opposite is more likely. Don't watch “The Big Short” too often.

That said, recession is not always as catastrophic as it was during the housing bubble.

On average, the S&P 500 lost 32% peak to trough during a recession. If we consider the median, and therefore exclude the most extreme values, it fell by 27% peak to trough. So, if we were facing a “normal” recession, we are already halfway there in terms of the decline. Even if the market were to fall by 15% in the coming months, I don't think it would be the end of the world.

Logically, we cannot know in advance if there will be a recession and how deep it will be, but I don't think it's appropriate to use a rare case like 2008-2009 (-57%) as a benchmark. That said, even if we were facing a new 2008, it doesn't mean we would suffer for years and years.

That recession was the longest since World War II and lasted 18 months. By averaging down during the recession, you could have reached the break-even point even sooner. Clearly, those who invested everything at the all-time high took years and years to recover their losses, but this is a basic mistake that should be avoided. If I am interested in a company or an index, I never invest everything at once, but divide the investment into 3-4 tranches, so I have the liquidity to average down.

Even in the worst-case scenario, after a year and a half of suffering, your portfolio will probably be back on track. If you have bet on companies with a terrible business model, then it's a different story, but that's up to you.

So the recession should be seen for what it is: an inevitable period of economic difficulty that will probably be resolved after a year or so. Either way, compared to 2008, American households seem to be in better shape financially, and that's important.

Household financial obligations have a lower weight on disposable personal income than in 2008, and credit card defaults are at a level that is not cause for concern. These figures have worsened in recent quarters, but this is nowhere near the levels seen in past recessions. If American households prove resilient, then consumption will not fall significantly in the event of a recession, and consequently the magnitude of the recession should not be much greater than the median.

The indicator that has never been wrong, so far

No one has a crystal ball, but there are indicators that are more reliable than others. A few weeks ago, I presented one that is little known but has so far proved infallible: the ratio between the yield on 3M T-Bills and the spread between high-yield bonds and the respective risk-free rate.

This indicator exploits bond market sentiment, as it spots the onset of a recession in advance. When the spread widens, it means that financial operators are demanding a higher premium for the risk they are taking, which is a typical behavior of those who do not trust the near future. At the same time, when the yield on 3M T-Bills falls, it means that the market is pricing in more rate cuts by the Fed, probably to cope with a more difficult economic environment.

When this ratio breaks below the 100-week moving average, it signals a recession is on the way.

This is the chart from a few weeks ago, and as you can see, there was a small downward break. However, in my article discussing this, I was cautious, as it could have been a false positive. For the signal to be valid, there must be a strong downward break.

Here's what happened after two weeks: I would say that today the break is evident. This time I also marked green lines that correspond to previous market bottoms, from which we can draw the following conclusion.

If the indicator proves reliable again, the bottom is still quite far away. Every time it has been reached, the black line was almost at the bottom (~0.10%), and today we are not even halfway there: there could still be room for a further drop.

Conclusion

Interest rates are fueling chaos, the yield curve and bond market spreads are signaling a recession. However, US households are not as heavily indebted as they were in 2008, which leads me to believe that they can maintain a certain degree of resilience even in the event of a recession. I would rule out a catastrophic scenario.

If I had to answer the question in the title, I would say that there is a higher probability that the bottom has not yet been reached. It has never been so difficult to make predictions because all it takes is one post to cause the S&P 500 to crash or rebound. The crucial issue is tariffs; without them, we probably would not have had this decline. The proof of this statement lies in the fact that the market recorded one of the best days in its history when Trump announced a pause.

That said, tariffs are here to stay, and I believe they will continue to be a widely used tool for Trump in negotiations. As long as this is the case, it is unlikely that the stock market will regain investor confidence. In particular, China is not willing to back down and has several levers at its disposal to win the challenge against the US; I discussed this in a previous article. The parties need to move closer together, but they are still quite far apart.

The ball is in China's court. China needs to make a deal with us; we don't have to make a deal with them. There's no difference between China and any other country except they are much larger, and China wants what we have... the American consumer. They need our money.

White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt

But if this is the tone, how can an agreement be reached?
